dialogue.utterance: Dispatch to Unit 3 for potential overdose at 1415 Broad Street.
Hello, I'm Emily, an EMT. We were called for an overdose. Can you tell me what's happening?
She's unconscious and not responding. I think she might have OD'd on Seroquel and Benadryl.
Understood. Any chance she might have used something else, like opioids or cocaine?
One of us thinks she might've used something in the last day-maybe opioids or even coke.
Testing responsiveness... Unresponsive to voice and pain. She doesn't react at all.
Her pupils are pinpoint and not reactive to light. This is concerning for opioid or drug use.
No, she's never looked this bad. She has a history of cocaine use but is typically healthy.
No movement with pain stimulation. She doesn't seem to feel anything.
Airway is patent with head tilt-chin lift. Slight vomitus in left nostril-no obstruction.
Shallow breathing, irregular-about 8 to 12 a minute. Looks like decreased respiratory drive and possible hypoxia.
Confirmed: 8-12 breaths per minute, shallow. Needs assisted ventilation.
SpO₂ is 68%. Low. She needs BVM support now for hypoxia.
Initiate BVM without oxygen attached. Watch for oxygen saturation improvement.
Pulse is strong, elevated. Skin is cool, pale, diaphoretic. Could indicate shock or overdose.
Pulse is 105, regular. Elevated heart rate, possible sinus tachycardia.
BP is 185/75. Elevated systolic, normal diastolic, but later it becomes hypotensive.
Need IV access for medication. Trying left arm first-extremity vein.
20-gauge IV placed in left antecubital. Flushed with 10 ml saline with no issues.
Checking her glucose before the Narcan for rule out of hypoglycemia.
Blood glucose is 225. No hypoglycemia present, so we can continue to administer Narcan.
Let's get a 4-lead EKG. Anything abnormal like arrhythmias or signs of heart strain?
EKG shows sinus tachycardia. No ST elevation or arrhythmia noted.
Skin is pale, cool, diaphoretic. No rashes or bite marks. Any visible trauma elsewhere?
No, she had no marks or injuries before we found her unconscious.
Pupils still pinpoint. Any stiffness or issues in the neck, like swollen glands or swelling?
No, no issues with the neck or abnormal movements noted before she passed out.
Vomitus in left nostril, no obstruction. Airway still patent after BVM use.
She was fine before going alone into the room. Found her unconscious after 10-20 minutes.
S.A.M.P.L.E. history-What medications is she currently on, or are there any known allergies?
She uses albuterol for her asthmatic condition-no other long-term meds or known allergies.
Last time she ate or drank?
Don't know exactly. She was alone for 10-20 minutes before we found her in this condition.
Any events before this that might've triggered it?
No obvious event-just a possible ingestion or overdose from multiple medications.
Since she's unresponsive with pinpoint pupils, following protocol 7-1 for opioid overdose. Narcan is the next step.
Administering 0.5 mg Narcan via IV now.
After Narcan, her pulse decreased from 105 to 99.
Respirations increased to 24 per minute and started to stabilize with assisted breathing.
BP is now unobtainable, 0/0. Minimal circulation or hypotension in progress.
SpO₂ is up to 99%, and she is now sitting up and showing agitation.
She is speaking. "I don't wanna die," repeated. She rocks and thrashes but is coherent-still confused.
She's agitated and moving, but no signs of typical severe pain. Pain level remains 0.
She pulled out the IV-can't administer repeat dose unless we can re-stage the line or use an alternate access.
Transporting to [COMPANY_NAME]. Calling hospital with initial vitals and documentation we have.
Manually lift her using limb lift from chair. Secure with 3 straps and raise two rails on cot.
She's secured on the cot with three straps. Still alert but agitated and trying to move.
Calling [COMPANY_NAME] now via my personal cell phone. Initial vitals and Narcan given. No ID from the patient yet.
She's trying to untie herself. Stay by her side and prevent her from removing the restraints.
She keeps moving and asking for help but denies giving personal information or confirming conditions.
Initial report from my phone. Hospital is ready and informed we are en route.
12-lead EKG indicates sinus tach. No cardiac changes or acute MI signs.
Sinus tach with occasional PVC noted. No acute myocardial changes.
She didn't mention being pregnant. Inputting "0" in gravida/para for placeholder.
IV was dislodged. Cannot give second dose. Confirming Larx status and documented in EMR.
Opioid protocol is completed. Transport ongoing. EKG and vitals updated.
No complications en route. Still heading to [COMPANY_NAME] as planned.
Patient is ready for handoff. Trauma Bed 9 confirmed and prepared.
Neck veins are not distended. No lymphadenopathy or neck masses observed.
No ST segment elevation or depression. Normal EKG for now.
She kept trying to remove her restraints. Remaining stable en route.
She has a history of severe asthma and cocaine use. Family confirmed that.
No vagal responses or allergic reactions noted. She did respond to Narcan, confirming opioid involvement.
Ending opioid overdose protocol. Continue monitoring during en route to [COMPANY_NAME].
She has no visible injuries. Family was not cooperative in providing additional patient info or ID.
She does not appear to be pregnant based on history, verbal, and physical cues.
She is not denying nor confirming pregnancy status. Documenting in the EMR accordingly.
Patient self-transfers to Trauma Bed 9 with assistance. Remains agitated, s/p Narcan.
A full patient report given to hospital staff once she moved into Trauma Bay 9.
Family did not provide any personal IDs or confirm patient info despite encouragement.
[COMPANY_NAME] was called and kept the family away from us during the transfer. They were uncooperative.
Repeat dose of Narcan was attempted but no IV access due to dislodged line. Documented in EPC as 'no second dose administered'.
Patient is uninterruptively agitated, not answering whether she is pregnant or giving Gravida/Para details.
She continues to actively attempt to unsecure the restraints. Nothing stopped during transport.
All vitals, medications, and care information have been uploaded to ImageTrend now.
Verified all documentation and that there were no complications while transporting to the trauma bay.
Hospital confirmed they have everything and no further action needed from the EMS crew.
Final notes for GCS on arrival were 6 (E1, V1, M4). She improved by handoff.
Handoff was complete. Patient is in Trauma Bay 9 at [COMPANY_NAME], now under hospital care.
Scene has been cleared, family kept at bay. We're on our way home without lights or sirens.
